metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or Remediation step) requiring a CLAUDE.md / ADR update that documents the new submodule ownership convention and the rule that logic must not live in `__init__.py`
findings.finding: The plan introduces a significant architectural reorganization (thinning a 1224-LOC god-facade into topical submodules) but specifies no documentation update — no ADR, no CLAUDE.md update, no design doc — to capture the new canonical symbol locations and the convention for future additions.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: '- [ ] CLAUDE.md (or equivalent design doc) updated to document the facade-thinning convention: new symbol bodies go in the owning topical submodule; `__init__.py` is re-export only.' This can be a one-paragraph addition to an existing architecture section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/architecture.md` (the `__init__.py` remedy-row) to the explicit list of files the plan must update, superseding the 'KEEP as one surface' rationale with the new split rationale.
findings.finding: The existing `docs/architecture.md` remedy table (line 220) carries an explicit architectural decision to 'KEEP [__init__.py] as one surface' with a specific justification ('a deliberate flat public-API namespace whose functions share private helpers; a read/write split forces re-exports for no readability gain'). The plan directly contradicts this documented decision without acknowledging or superseding it. Consumers reading the architecture doc will see a contradiction after the split.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the plan: remove `src/rebar/__init__.py` from `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt` (and update the `docs/architecture.md` remedy row) as part of the split, and add any newly-over-cap submodule to the allowlist with a remedy row.
findings.finding: The plan's split will require updating the module-size allowlist (`.github/module-size-allowlist.txt`) and the `docs/architecture.md` remedy table — neither is mentioned. The structural test `tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py` asserts SET EQUALITY between the computed over-cap set and the allowlist; after the split `src/rebar/__init__.py` will drop below 800 LOC and must be removed from the allowlist, or the test fails in the STALE direction.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'housekeeping' step to the plan: (1) remove the `__init__.py` line from `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt`; (2) update the `docs/architecture.md` remedy table row for `__init__.py` to describe its new ceiling state; (3) add any new submodule >800 LOC to the allowlist.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly where cross-cutting private helpers (`_python_leaf`, `_json_or`) land after the split — either name a target submodule (e.g. `_commands/_seam.py` for `_python_leaf`) or permit them to stay in `__init__.py` with a documented rationale that they are glue, not business logic.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify where private cross-cutting helpers _python_leaf (line 511, used by 8+ public functions as a shared CommandError→RebarError trampoline) and _json_or (line 356, a JSON-parse sentinel) land after the split. Both are ineligible for any single owning submodule and have no obvious topical home. Without placement guidance the implementer may orphan them in __init__.py (defeating the thinning goal), duplicate them across submodules, or create a sub-100-LOC fragment — all of which violate the plan's own AC.
findings.suggested_fix: _python_leaf is a CommandError→RebarError adapter that already belongs conceptually alongside _commands._seam (which owns CommandError); move it there or to a new _commands/_lib.py if _seam is the wrong layer. _json_or is only used by clarity_check; move it inline or as a module-private in the target gates cluster.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `rebar._json_or` as an unenumerated consumer of a private __init__ symbol; specify whether it is re-exported on the `rebar` namespace post-split or the test is updated.
findings.finding: `rebar._json_or` is accessed directly by an external test consumer (`tests/unit/test_json_or_narrowing.py`) using `rebar._json_or(...)` — three call sites. `_json_or` is a private helper defined in `__init__.py`, not listed in `__all__`. The plan does not enumerate this consumer, and if `_json_or` is relocated to a submodule and not re-exported on `rebar`, the test breaks.
findings.suggested_fix: Either keep `_json_or` in __init__.py (it's a 10-line helper with no submodule home) or add it explicitly to the re-export surface with a note that `rebar._json_or` is a pinned semi-public test contract.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC #2) to explicitly verify that `__all__` is updated in every affected submodule and that any intra-package imports of moved symbols still resolve (e.g. via a full import smoke-test of all submodules).
findings.finding: The cascade_flag calls out work items — verifying star-imports, checking `__all__` integrity, and auditing `rebar._<oldloc>` direct internal imports — but no Acceptance Criterion covers correctness of `__all__` or internal import paths after the move. AC #2 only checks the public `rebar.<fn>` surface; it does not verify that `__all__` lists are updated or that intra-package imports that previously pointed at `__init__.py` symbols are redirected.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that explicitly verifies `__all__` is updated and `dir(rebar)` is unchanged post-split, and specify the resolution policy for any discovered direct `rebar._<submodule>` imports (fix-forward, document as breaking, or add compatibility shims).
findings.finding: The cascade_flag names three verification concerns (direct `rebar._<oldloc>` imports, star-imports, `__all__`, `dir(rebar)` stability) but none of the Acceptance Criteria include a verifiable end-state check for these — the plan says 'check' without specifying the resolution or how success is confirmed.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '`__all__` in `__init__.py` enumerates every previously public name; any file found importing `rebar._<submodule>.fn` directly is updated to use the canonical `rebar.<fn>` path; a grep/AST scan confirms no remaining direct private-path imports of relocated symbols.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Finding section: replace `_writes` with the actual write-path delegation targets (`_commands`, `_io`, `signing`, `_engine_support`) so the executor knows which existing submodules to relocate write bodies into.
findings.finding: The plan's Finding section asserts the facade has '~49 top-level defs mostly delegating to `_reads`/`_commands`/`_writes`', naming `_writes` as an existing owning submodule. No `src/rebar/_writes.py` or `rebar._writes` package exists; the write path in __init__.py delegates to `_commands` (composer, leaf, session_log, unlink, compact, fsck, fsck_recover), `_io` (export_ndjson, import_ndjson), `signing`, and `_engine_support`. The only `_writes`-named file is `_mcp_writes.py`, which is the MCP server write surface — not the __init__.py delegation target.
findings.location: Finding section: '~49 top-level defs mostly delegating to `_reads`/`_commands`/`_writes`'
findings.suggested_fix: Update the Finding sentence to read: '…~49 top-level defs mostly delegating to `_reads`/`_commands`/`_io`/`signing`/`_engine_support`' and remove the non-existent `_writes` module reference.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete LOC ceiling for __init__.py directly in this plan (e.g., '≤50 LOC') so AC1 is self-contained and measurable without consulting S1.
findings.finding: The ceiling value for __init__.py is not stated in this plan — it is delegated to an external 'S1 ratchet ticket'. AC1 ('under the pinned ceiling') is therefore unverifiable without resolving a dependency not included here.
findings.location: Remediation — 'pin __init__.py to a ceiling row (see S1 ratchet ticket)' and AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'under the pinned ceiling' with the actual number, e.g. '`__init__.py` must be ≤60 LOC (imports + re-exports only); enforce with `wc -l src/rebar/__init__.py | awk \'$1 > 60 {exit 1}\'`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for each AC: (AC1) a LOC gate command; (AC2) a `python -c "import rebar; assert set(dir(rebar)) == BASELINE"` snapshot diff; (AC3) a `find`/`wc` pipeline asserting no new file is <100 LOC.
findings.finding: No concrete proving command is provided for any of the three ACs; all three lack the command needed to produce evidence on success.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — all three bullet points
findings.suggested_fix:
AC2 proving command: `python - <<'EOF'
import rebar, json, pathlib
baseline = set(json.loads(pathlib.Path('rebar_dir_baseline.json').read_text()))
assert set(dir(rebar)) == baseline, set(dir(rebar)) ^ baseline
EOF`
Capture baseline before the refactor with `python -c "import rebar,json; print(json.dumps(dir(rebar)))" > rebar_dir_baseline.json`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC verifying that a size-gate ceiling row for `__init__.py` is added at or below a stated LOC limit (e.g. '`__init__.py` ceiling row ≤ 50 LOC is present in the S1 ratchet config').
findings.finding: The Remediation describes a concrete deliverable — 'pin `__init__.py` to a ceiling row (see S1 ratchet ticket)' — that has no covering Acceptance Criterion. AC #1 says only that `__init__.py` is 'a re-export surface under the pinned ceiling' but does not verify that the ceiling row is actually added or what the numeric ceiling is, leaving the ratchet step unverifiable.
findings.location: Remediation section ('pin `__init__.py` to a ceiling row'); AC #1
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '`__init__.py` has a ratchet ceiling row set to ≤ 50 LOC (or the post-split actual count) in the S1 size-gate config file.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit symbol-to-submodule assignment table (or grouping rule keyed to existing file names) so the executor need not infer cluster membership independently.
findings.finding: The plan defers the symbol-to-submodule assignment to the executor: 'clusters that already hang together' and 'their existing owning submodules' are undefined. With ~49 symbols, the executor must independently decide which of _reads/_commands/_writes (and potentially others) each function belongs to, creating a real clarification-or-guesswork stop.
findings.location: Remediation section — 'Split only along clusters that already hang together'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a section: 'Cluster assignment: functions currently delegating to _reads go to _reads.py; those delegating to _commands go to _commands.py; those delegating to _writes go to _writes.py; any with no existing owning module go to a new _misc.py if it will be ≥100 LOC, else stay in __init__.py.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing (e.g. keep re-export shims in `__init__.py` for one release cycle, or document a git-revert procedure) to address the acknowledged risk of breaking direct submodule imports.
findings.finding: The plan acknowledges the risk of breaking `rebar._<oldloc>` direct imports (cascade_flag) but provides no rollback path, back-out plan, or expand-contract sequencing. The T4 criterion requires an explicit reversibility mechanism — acknowledgement alone is insufficient.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' section: 'If post-merge CI or smoke-tests reveal a broken import, revert commit <SHA>. Optionally, phase the split by first adding re-export shims in each submodule alongside the existing __init__.py bodies, verify green, then remove the __init__.py bodies in a follow-up PR.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale to the Remediation section explaining why relocating into existing owning submodules is preferred (e.g. 'avoids a new synthetic intermediary, uses already-topical module boundaries, and keeps the blast radius confined to files that already own the relevant logic').
findings.finding: The plan states no positive rationale for why relocating bodies into existing topical submodules is the right approach — it describes WHAT to do and lists constraints but never says WHY this structure fits better than alternatives (e.g. a single _facade.py intermediary, or keeping bodies and adding a LOC suppression). The absence of a rationale is a G6 requirement gap.
findings.suggested_fix: Insert after the first Remediation sentence: 'This approach is preferred because the owning submodules already carry the relevant domain logic, avoiding a new synthetic intermediary (e.g. _facade.py) that would merely shift the coupling without improving discoverability or testability.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
